conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. To assess your eligibility, I'll need to ask you some questions about your circumstances.

Let me first review the official eligibility rules so I can guide you properly.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for waiting. Now let me help you determine your eligibility for Child Benefit.

To get started, could you tell me: **Do you have any children, and if so, how many?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I'd like to claim Child Benefit. I have one child - my son Alex. He's 8 years old and lives with me here in Manchester.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! So you have one child, Alex, who is 8 years old and lives with you in Manchester.

Let me ask you a few more questions to assess your eligibility:

**Are you currently living in the UK?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK - in Manchester, as I mentioned.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect, thank you for confirming that.

Now, to ensure I have all the information needed: **Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex, or is there anyone else who might also consider themselves responsible for him (such as another parent or guardian)?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex. It's just me looking after him.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for confirming that. Just a couple more questions:

**Are you responsible for Alex - meaning you live with him and provide for his needs such as food, clothes, and other expenses?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I am responsible for Alex. He lives with me and I provide for his needs.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Excellent. Just one final question to complete my assessment:

**Is Alex currently in hospital, care, or any residential accommodation, or has he been for an extended period?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Well, actually yes - Alex has been in hospital for a few months now. It's been quite difficult.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
I'm sorry to hear that Alex has been in hospital - that must be very difficult for you and your family.

To assess how this affects your Child Benefit eligibility, I need to know:

**How many weeks has Alex been in hospital, and do you regularly spend money on him while he's there (for example, on clothes, toys, treats, or other items for him)?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: He's been in hospital for about 15 weeks now. Unfortunately, I haven't been able to afford to spend money on his needs while he's been in there - it's been really tough financially.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for sharing that information with me, and I'm very sorry to hear about the difficult situation you're facing.

Based on the information you've provided, I now have enough details to assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for Alex. Let me provide you with my assessment:

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Assessment Result: Unfortunately, you are not currently eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.**

Here's why:

Under normal circumstances, you would qualify for Child Benefit because:
- Alex is under 16 years old
- You live in the UK
- You are responsible for him and he normally lives with you
- Nobody else is claiming for him

However, when a child is in hospital for **more than 12 weeks**, Child Benefit entitlement usually stops after that time limit. Since Alex has been in hospital for 15 weeks, this affects your eligibility.

There is an **exception** to this rule: you can continue to receive Child Benefit if you regularly spend money on your child while they're in hospital (for example, on clothes, toys, treats, or other items for them). Unfortunately, as you've mentioned you haven't been able to afford to spend money on Alex's needs during his hospital stay, this exception doesn't apply in your situation.

I understand this is a difficult time for you and your family, both emotionally and financially. If Alex returns home from hospital, you would be able to claim Child Benefit again at that point. You may also want to check if there are other forms of support available to you during this challenging period.

If you have any questions or if circumstances change, please don't hesitate to reach out.

claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 8 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 8 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child in hospital/residential accommodation for 15 weeks (>12) and claimant is not regularly spending money on child
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Child in hospital/residential accommodation for 15 weeks (>12) and claimant is not regularly spending money on child
circumstances: Child is not in local authority care
Child in hospital/residential accommodation for 15 weeks (>12) and claimant is not regularly spending money on child
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

reasonings: Alex is 8 years old and normally lives with his parent in the UK, which would typically make the parent eligible for Child Benefit. However, according to UK Child Benefit rules, if a child is in hospital for more than 12 weeks, the parent is no longer entitled to Child Benefit after that time limit. There is an exception to this rule: if the parent regularly spends money on the child while they are in hospital (for example on clothes, toys, treats, or other items), Child Benefit can continue. In this case, Alex has been in hospital for 15 weeks (which exceeds the 12-week limit), and the parent has confirmed they have not been able to afford to spend money on Alex's needs while he has been in hospital. Therefore, the exception does not apply, and the parent would not be eligible for Child Benefit for Alex at this time.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant has one child, Alex (8 years old), who normally lives with them in Manchester, UK. While the claimant meets the basic eligibility criteria (responsible for a child under 16, living in the UK, living with the child), Alex has been in hospital for 15 weeks. Under Child Benefit rules, entitlement stops after 12 weeks in hospital unless the parent regularly spends money on the child. As the claimant has not been able to afford to spend money on Alex while he's been in hospital, they are not currently eligible for Child Benefit.

However, according to UK Child Benefit rules, if a child is in hospital for more than 12 weeks, the parent is no longer entitled to Child Benefit after that time limit. There is an exception to this rule: if the parent regularly spends money on the child while they are in hospital (for example on clothes, toys, treats, or other items), Child Benefit can continue. In this case, Alex has been in hospital for 15 weeks (which exceeds the 12-week limit), and the parent has confirmed they have not been able to afford to spend money on Alex's needs while he has been in hospital. Therefore, the exception does not apply, and the parent would not be eligible for Child Benefit for Alex at this time.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant has one child, Alex (8 years old), who normally lives with them in Manchester, UK. While the claimant meets the basic eligibility criteria (responsible for a child under 16, living in the UK, living with the child), Alex has been in hospital for 15 weeks. Under Child Benefit rules, entitlement stops after 12 weeks in hospital unless the parent regularly spends money on the child. As the claimant has not been able to afford to spend money on Alex while he's been in hospital, they are not currently eligible for Child Benefit.
